front: Under Michigan v. Summers (1981), what detention authority does a premises search warrant implicitly carry?
back: The limited authority to detain the occupants of the premises while a proper search is conducted — no individualized suspicion of the occupant is required; the authority rides on the warrant itself.

front: Summers (1981): "a warrant to search for contraband founded on probable cause implicitly carries with it the {{c1::limited authority to detain the occupants of the premises}} while a proper search is conducted."
back: limited authority to detain the occupants of the premises

front: Is individualized suspicion of an occupant required to detain him under Michigan v. Summers during execution of a premises search warrant?
back: No. The Summers detention is categorical — it requires no individualized suspicion of the occupant; the authority flows from the search warrant itself.

front: Under Muehler v. Mena (2005), how far may a Summers detention go in terms of restraint?
back: Occupants may be handcuffed and detained for the entire duration of the search where the governmental safety interest outweighs the marginal intrusion; here a 2-to-3-hour handcuffed detention during a search of a gang house for weapons by two officers was reasonable.

front: Which case upheld handcuffing four detainees for 2-to-3 hours during a search of a suspected gang house for weapons by two officers?
back: Muehler v. Mena (2005) — the Court held the handcuffed detention for the length of the search was reasonable and consistent with Michigan v. Summers.

front: Under Muehler v. Mena, did questioning Mena about her immigration status during the lawful detention require separate Fourth Amendment justification?
back: No. Because the questioning did not constitute an independent Fourth Amendment seizure, no additional Fourth Amendment justification was required — questioning during a lawful detention that does not prolong it is not a fresh seizure.

front: Muehler v. Mena (2005): "because the questioning in this case did not constitute an {{c1::independent Fourth Amendment seizure}} ... no additional Fourth Amendment justification for inquiring about Mena's immigration status was required."
back: independent Fourth Amendment seizure

front: Under Bailey v. United States (2013), what spatial limit confines the Summers detention authority?
back: Summers detention is limited to the immediate vicinity of the premises to be searched; it does not authorize detaining a former occupant who left before the search and is stopped at a distance (here about a mile away).

front: Bailey v. United States (2013): "A {{c1::spatial constraint}} defined by the {{c2::immediate vicinity of the premises to be searched}} is therefore required for detentions incident to the execution of a search warrant."
back: c1: spatial constraint; c2: immediate vicinity of the premises to be searched

front: Once an occupant has left and is stopped beyond the immediate vicinity of the searched premises, what must justify the stop (Bailey v. United States)?
back: Summers no longer applies — the stop must rest on independent reasonable suspicion (Terry) or probable cause; the search warrant alone will not carry it.

front: Distinguish: when does Summers authorize detaining an occupant, and where does Bailey cut that authority off?
back: Summers permits suspicionless detention of occupants in the immediate vicinity during execution of a premises search warrant; Bailey holds the authority does not reach a former occupant stopped beyond that immediate vicinity (e.g., a mile away), who then needs independent reasonable suspicion or probable cause.

front: State the two prongs of a Maryland v. Buie (1990) protective sweep.
back: (1) Without probable cause or reasonable suspicion, officers may as a precaution look in closets and other spaces immediately adjoining the place of arrest from which an attack could be immediately launched; (2) beyond that, there must be articulable facts that, with rational inferences, would warrant a reasonably prudent officer in believing the area to be swept harbors an individual posing a danger to those on the arrest scene.

front: Under Buie's first prong, what may officers look into without any probable cause or reasonable suspicion incident to an in-home arrest?
back: Closets and other spaces immediately adjoining the place of arrest from which an attack could be immediately launched.

front: Under Buie's second prong, what is required for a protective sweep beyond the spaces immediately adjoining the place of arrest?
back: Articulable facts that, taken with rational inferences, would warrant a reasonably prudent officer in believing the area to be swept harbors an individual posing a danger to those on the arrest scene.

front: What is a Buie protective sweep (and what is it NOT)?
back: A cursory visual inspection of places a person could hide, justified by officer-safety danger — it is a search for people, NOT a search for evidence.

front: Common pitfall: how does an officer turn a lawful Buie protective sweep into an unlawful search?
back: By treating it as a full search — opening drawers or 'sweeping' for contraband. A Buie sweep is only a quick look for people in spaces where a person could hide; rummaging for evidence exceeds it and converts it into an unlawful search.

front: Under Illinois v. McArthur (2001), what may police do to a home while obtaining a search warrant?
back: With probable cause to believe the home contains contraband, police may impose a brief, reasonable freeze — preventing a resident from re-entering unaccompanied for a limited time (here about two hours) — to prevent destruction of evidence.

front: Which case held that barring a man with hidden marijuana from re-entering his home for about two hours while police obtained a search warrant was reasonable?
back: Illinois v. McArthur (2001) — the Court concluded the officers acted reasonably and did not violate the Fourth Amendment.

front: Is the McArthur 'freeze' of a residence pending a warrant a search?
back: No. It is a limited, temporary restraint to preserve evidence — not a search.

front: Under Segura v. United States (1984), is evidence seized under a later warrant admissible despite a prior illegal entry?
back: Yes — where the warrant was supported wholly by information known to police independent of (and before) the unlawful entry, the evidence later seized under that warrant is admissible.

front: How do McArthur and Segura approach securing premises while a warrant issues from opposite directions?
back: McArthur permits the freeze itself as a reasonable restraint to prevent evidence destruction; Segura is the companion from the other direction — securing premises while a warrant issues does not poison evidence later seized under a warrant grounded on wholly independent information.

front: Under United States v. Conner (8th Cir. 1997, persuasive), what is it when officers demand a door be opened under color of authority and the occupant complies?
back: A warrantless constructive entry/search — not a consensual encounter — violating the Fourth Amendment absent consent or exigent circumstances. (Eighth Circuit precedent, persuasive only — not a nationwide rule.)
cite: United States v. Conner, 127 F.3d 663, 667 (8th Cir. 1997)

front: United States v. Conner (8th Cir. 1997, persuasive): "an unconstitutional search occurs when officers gain visual or physical access to a motel room after an occupant opens the door {{c1::not voluntarily, but in response to a demand under color of authority}}."
back: not voluntarily, but in response to a demand under color of authority
cite: United States v. Conner, 127 F.3d 663, 667 (8th Cir. 1997)

front: Is the Conner constructive-entry doctrine a seizure-of-the-person rule, and under what framework is it analyzed?
back: No — it is a constructive-entry / unlawful-search doctrine under the warrant requirement, not a seizure-of-the-person rule. The surround-and-call-out problem is analyzed as a Payton/Steagald entry question, not under consent doctrine.
cite: United States v. Conner, 127 F.3d 663, 666 (8th Cir. 1997)

front: Common pitfall: officers command occupants to open the door under color of authority and the door opens — why is calling this a consensual 'knock-and-talk' wrong?
back: Because compliance with a demand under color of authority is a constructive entry (Conner, 8th Cir. persuasive), not consent. Lawfulness turns on the entry rules of Payton/Steagald, not on consent doctrine.
cite: United States v. Conner, 127 F.3d 663 (8th Cir. 1997)

front: Under Payton v. New York (1980), what does an arrest warrant authorize as to entering the suspect's own home?
back: An arrest warrant founded on probable cause implicitly carries the limited authority to enter a dwelling in which the suspect lives when there is reason to believe the suspect is within.

front: Under Steagald v. United States (1981), what is required to enter a THIRD PARTY's home to arrest the subject of an arrest warrant?
back: A search warrant — absent exigent circumstances or consent. An arrest warrant alone protects the suspect, not the third-party homeowner.

front: Distinguish Payton from Steagald on what warrant is needed to enter a home for an arrest-warrant subject.
back: Payton: an arrest warrant alone authorizes entry into the SUSPECT'S OWN home when there is reason to believe he is inside. Steagald: entering a THIRD PARTY'S home for that suspect requires a separate SEARCH warrant (absent exigency or consent).

front: What are the three core 'securing the scene' authorities and their anchoring cases?
back: (1) Detain occupants during a premises search (Summers, extended to handcuffing in Muehler); (2) protective sweep incident to an in-home arrest (Buie); (3) brief freeze of a residence with probable cause while a warrant is obtained (McArthur).

front: Why does the Summers detention authority exist (what interests support detaining occupants during a search)?
back: It rests on legitimate law-enforcement interests served during execution of a premises search warrant — including officer safety and the orderly completion of the search — which justify a categorical, suspicionless detention of occupants in the immediate vicinity.
